flashcards.back: A modern software development methodology that emphasizes flexibility, collaboration, iterative development, and rapid delivery of working software. It responds to changing requirements throughout the development cycle.

flashcards.front: When was the Agile Manifesto formulated and by whom?
flashcards.back: The Agile Manifesto was formulated in 2001 by 17 software developers at The Lodge at Snowbird, Utah, who sought better ways to develop software.

flashcards.front: What are the 4 values of the Agile Manifesto?
flashcards.back: 1) Individuals and interactions over processes and tools, 2) Working software over comprehensive documentation, 3) Customer collaboration over contract negotiation, 4) Responding to change over following a plan.

flashcards.front: What is the first principle of Agile?
flashcards.back: Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software.

flashcards.front: What is iterative development in Agile?
flashcards.back: A development approach where software is built in small, incremental cycles called iterations or sprints, typically lasting 1-4 weeks, allowing for feedback and adaptation.

flashcards.front: What is a Sprint in Agile/Scrum?
flashcards.back: A time-boxed iteration (usually 2-4 weeks) during which a potentially releasable product increment is created. It has a defined start and end date.
A prioritized list of features, functions, requirements, enhancements, and fixes that need to be developed. It is owned by the Product Owner and is dynamic.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Agile and Waterfall?
flashcards.back: Waterfall is sequential and rigid with phases flowing downwards; Agile is iterative, flexible, and welcomes changing requirements even late in development.

flashcards.front: What are the three roles in Scrum framework?
flashcards.back: 1) Product Owner - defines features and prioritizes backlog, 2) Scrum Master - facilitates the process and removes impediments, 3) Development Team - cross-functional members who deliver the product.
A short, simple description of a feature from the end-user's perspective, typically following the format: 'As a [user], I want [goal] so that [benefit].
A 15-minute daily meeting where team members share what they did yesterday, what they will do today, and any blockers they face.
A meeting held at the end of each sprint where the team reflects on what went well, what didn't, and identifies improvements for the next sprint.
A visual workflow management method that uses a Kanban board to visualize work, limit work-in-progress (WIP), and maximize efficiency.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Scrum and XP (Extreme Programming)?
flashcards.back: Scrum focuses on management and delivery cycles; XP focuses on engineering practices like pair programming, test-driven development, and continuous integration.

flashcards.front: Why is Agile preferred for projects with uncertain requirements?
flashcards.back: Agile embraces change through iterative cycles, allowing customers to provide feedback frequently and enabling the team to adapt to changing requirements without major disruptions.
